• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Predicting Advertising Spend: From Poisson to Negative Binomial Regression

Poisson Regression with Overdispersion Correction via Negative Binomial

Analysis Report

2026-04-26

1 Introduction

This report models local advertising spend across 400 retail carseat locations using data from the Carseats dataset in the ISLR package. The analysis follows a two-stage approach:

  1. We first fit a Poisson regression model, which is the natural starting point for count data.
  2. We then diagnose the Poisson model and find significant overdispersion (variance far exceeding the mean), which violates a core Poisson assumption and inflates the apparent significance of predictors.
  3. We address this by refitting using a Negative Binomial regression model, which relaxes the mean-variance equality constraint and produces more reliable standard errors and inference.

Response variable: Advertising, the local advertising budget (in thousands of dollars) at each store location.

Predictors selected: Sales, Income, Price, and US, chosen through correlation analysis and ANOVA testing (detailed in Section 4).

2 Setup

library(ISLR)
library(tidymodels)
library(tidyverse)
library(GGally)
library(car)
library(broom)
library(gridExtra)
library(poissonreg)
library(MASS)
data(Carseats)
glimpse(Carseats)
## Rows: 400
## Columns: 11
## $ Sales       <dbl> 9.50, 11.22, 10.06, 7.40, 4.15, 10.81, 6.63, 11.85, 6.54, …
## $ CompPrice   <dbl> 138, 111, 113, 117, 141, 124, 115, 136, 132, 132, 121, 117…
## $ Income      <dbl> 73, 48, 35, 100, 64, 113, 105, 81, 110, 113, 78, 94, 35, 2…
## $ Advertising <dbl> 11, 16, 10, 4, 3, 13, 0, 15, 0, 0, 9, 4, 2, 11, 11, 5, 0, …
## $ Population  <dbl> 276, 260, 269, 466, 340, 501, 45, 425, 108, 131, 150, 503,…
## $ Price       <dbl> 120, 83, 80, 97, 128, 72, 108, 120, 124, 124, 100, 94, 136…
## $ ShelveLoc   <fct> Bad, Good, Medium, Medium, Bad, Bad, Medium, Good, Medium,…
## $ Age         <dbl> 42, 65, 59, 55, 38, 78, 71, 67, 76, 76, 26, 50, 62, 53, 52…
## $ Education   <dbl> 17, 10, 12, 14, 13, 16, 15, 10, 10, 17, 10, 13, 18, 18, 18…
## $ Urban       <fct> Yes, Yes, Yes, Yes, Yes, No, Yes, Yes, No, No, No, Yes, Ye…
## $ US          <fct> Yes, Yes, Yes, Yes, No, Yes, No, Yes, No, Yes, Yes, Yes, N…
summary(Carseats)
##      Sales          CompPrice       Income        Advertising    
##  Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 77   Min.   : 21.00   Min.   : 0.000  
##  1st Qu.: 5.390   1st Qu.:115   1st Qu.: 42.75   1st Qu.: 0.000  
##  Median : 7.490   Median :125   Median : 69.00   Median : 5.000  
##  Mean   : 7.496   Mean   :125   Mean   : 68.66   Mean   : 6.635  
##  3rd Qu.: 9.320   3rd Qu.:135   3rd Qu.: 91.00   3rd Qu.:12.000  
##  Max.   :16.270   Max.   :175   Max.   :120.00   Max.   :29.000  
##    Population        Price        ShelveLoc        Age          Education   
##  Min.   : 10.0   Min.   : 24.0   Bad   : 96   Min.   :25.00   Min.   :10.0  
##  1st Qu.:139.0   1st Qu.:100.0   Good  : 85   1st Qu.:39.75   1st Qu.:12.0  
##  Median :272.0   Median :117.0   Medium:219   Median :54.50   Median :14.0  
##  Mean   :264.8   Mean   :115.8                Mean   :53.32   Mean   :13.9  
##  3rd Qu.:398.5   3rd Qu.:131.0                3rd Qu.:66.00   3rd Qu.:16.0  
##  Max.   :509.0   Max.   :191.0                Max.   :80.00   Max.   :18.0  
##  Urban       US     
##  No :118   No :142  
##  Yes:282   Yes:258  
##                     
##                     
##                     
##

3 Why Poisson Regression?

3.1 The Poisson Distribution

Poisson regression models count data by assuming the response variable \(Y\) follows a Poisson distribution:

\[P(Y = y) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^y}{y!}, \quad y = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\]

where \(\lambda = E(Y) = \text{Var}(Y)\) is both the mean and variance of the distribution. The model links predictors to this mean through a log link function:

\[\log(\hat{\lambda}_i) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_{i1} + \hat{\beta}_2 x_{i2} + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p x_{ip}\]

which is equivalent to:

\[\hat{\lambda}_i = e^{\hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_{i1} + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p x_{ip}}\]

3.2 Why Not Linear Regression?

Linear regression is inappropriate for Advertising for three reasons:

  • Advertising takes only non-negative integer values (0 to 29). Linear regression can predict negative values, which are meaningless here.
  • The distribution of Advertising is right-skewed with a spike at zero, violating the normality assumption of linear regression.
  • The variance increases with the mean in count data, which is better handled by the Poisson family.

3.3 Checking the Response Variable

cat("Mean of Advertising:    ", round(mean(Carseats$Advertising), 3), "\n")
## Mean of Advertising:     6.635
cat("Variance of Advertising:", round(var(Carseats$Advertising),  3), "\n")
## Variance of Advertising: 44.227
cat("Zeros in Advertising:   ", sum(Carseats$Advertising == 0), "\n")
## Zeros in Advertising:    144
cat("Max Advertising:        ", max(Carseats$Advertising), "\n")
## Max Advertising:         29
p1 <- ggplot(Carseats, aes(x = Advertising)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "steelblue", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(Carseats$Advertising),
             color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Distribution of Advertising Spend",
    subtitle = "Right-skewed with a spike at zero, consistent with count data",
    x = "Advertising Budget (thousands)", y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

p2 <- ggplot(Carseats, aes(x = log1p(Advertising))) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "steelblue", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Log(Advertising + 1) Distribution",
    subtitle = "Log transformation reveals more symmetric underlying structure",
    x = "log(Advertising + 1)", y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2)

The mean (6.64) and variance (44.23) are not equal, which indicates overdispersion relative to the strict Poisson assumption. This is common in real data and will be noted in the diagnostics section.

4 Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 Advertising vs Numeric Predictors

num_vars <- Carseats %>% dplyr::select_if(is.numeric)

cor_with_adv <- cor(num_vars)[, "Advertising"] %>%
  round(2) %>%
  enframe(name = "Variable", value = "Correlation") %>%
  dplyr::filter(Variable != "Advertising") %>%
  arrange(Correlation) %>%
  mutate(
    Direction = ifelse(Correlation > 0, "Positive", "Negative"),
    Variable  = fct_inorder(Variable)
  )

cor_with_adv %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(Correlation))) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Pearson Correlations with Advertising (sorted by absolute strength)",
    col.names = c("Predictor", "Correlation (r)", "Direction")
  )
Pearson Correlations with Advertising (sorted by absolute strength)
Predictor Correlation (r) Direction
Sales 0.27 Positive
Population 0.27 Positive
Income 0.06 Positive
Price 0.04 Positive
Education -0.03 Negative
CompPrice -0.02 Negative
Age 0.00 Negative 
ggplot(cor_with_adv, aes(x = Variable, y = Correlation, fill = Direction)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = Correlation, vjust = ifelse(Correlation >= 0, -0.4, 1.3)),
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Positive = "#2196F3", Negative = "#F44336")) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.6, 0.6)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Pearson Correlation of Each Numeric Predictor with Advertising",
    subtitle = "Sales shows the strongest positive association",
    x = NULL, y = "Pearson r"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1)
  )

Finding: Sales and Population share the strongest positive correlation with Advertising (both r = 0.27). Income, Price, and all remaining numeric predictors show near-zero correlations with Advertising. The dominant signal among numeric predictors is Sales.

4.2 Advertising vs Categorical Predictors

anova_results <- tibble(
  Variable = c("ShelveLoc", "US", "Urban"),
  F_statistic = c(
    summary(aov(Advertising ~ ShelveLoc, data = Carseats))[[1]][["F value"]][1],
    summary(aov(Advertising ~ US,        data = Carseats))[[1]][["F value"]][1],
    summary(aov(Advertising ~ Urban,     data = Carseats))[[1]][["F value"]][1]
  ),
  p_value = c(
    summary(aov(Advertising ~ ShelveLoc, data = Carseats))[[1]][["Pr(>F)"]][1],
    summary(aov(Advertising ~ US,        data = Carseats))[[1]][["Pr(>F)"]][1],
    summary(aov(Advertising ~ Urban,     data = Carseats))[[1]][["Pr(>F)"]][1]
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    Significant = ifelse(p_value < 0.05, "Yes", "No"),
    F_statistic = round(F_statistic, 2),
    p_value     = round(p_value, 4)
  )

knitr::kable(anova_results,
  caption = "One-Way ANOVA: Categorical Predictors vs Advertising",
  col.names = c("Variable", "F Statistic", "p-value", "Significant (alpha = 0.05)")
)
One-Way ANOVA: Categorical Predictors vs Advertising
Variable F Statistic p-value Significant (alpha = 0.05)
ShelveLoc 0.71 0.4946 No
US 350.80 0.0000 Yes
Urban 0.70 0.4018 No 
p_shelve <- ggplot(Carseats, aes(x = ShelveLoc, y = Advertising, fill = ShelveLoc)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Bad = "#F44336", Medium = "#FF9800", Good = "#4CAF50")) +
  labs(title = "Advertising by Shelf Location",
       x = "Shelf Location", y = "Advertising (thousands)") +
  theme_bw()

p_us <- ggplot(Carseats, aes(x = US, y = Advertising, fill = US)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(No = "#90CAF9", Yes = "#1565C0")) +
  labs(title = "Advertising by US Store",
       x = "US Store", y = "Advertising (thousands)") +
  theme_bw()

p_urban <- ggplot(Carseats, aes(x = Urban, y = Advertising, fill = Urban)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(No = "#CE93D8", Yes = "#6A1B9A")) +
  labs(title = "Advertising by Urban Location",
       x = "Urban", y = "Advertising (thousands)") +
  theme_bw()

grid.arrange(p_shelve, p_us, p_urban, ncol = 3)

Finding: US is the only significant categorical predictor (F = 350.80, p < 0.001), with US-based stores spending considerably more on advertising. Both ShelveLoc and Urban are non-significant (p > 0.05) and are excluded from the model.

4.3 Pairwise Relationships

Carseats %>%
  dplyr::select(Advertising, Sales, Price, Income, US) %>%
  ggpairs(
    aes(color = US, alpha = 0.4),
    upper = list(continuous = wrap("cor", size = 3)),
    lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8)),
    diag  = list(continuous = wrap("densityDiag", alpha = 0.5))
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  ggtitle("Pairwise Relationships Among Final Model Variables")

5 Model Specification

5.1 Predictor Justification

The model uses four predictors:

Predictor Type Justification
Sales Numeric Strongest correlation with Advertising (r = 0.27); stores with higher sales invest more in advertising
Income Numeric Small positive association (r = 0.06); included for contextual relevance as a market-level economic indicator
Price Numeric Near-zero correlation (r = 0.04); included as a product-level control variable
US Categorical (2 levels) Highly significant ANOVA result (F = 350.80, p < 0.001); US stores advertise substantially more

ShelveLoc and Urban were excluded as non-significant in ANOVA (p > 0.05). Age, Education, CompPrice, and Population were excluded based on near-zero correlations.

5.2 Poisson Model Equation

The initial Poisson regression model is expressed as:

\[\log(\hat{\lambda}_i) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot \text{Sales} + \hat{\beta}_2 \cdot \text{Income} + \hat{\beta}_3 \cdot \text{Price} + \hat{\beta}_4 \cdot \text{USYes}\]

where \(\hat{\lambda}_i\) is the expected advertising spend for store \(i\) and US uses No as the reference level.

6 Data Splitting

The dataset is split into 80% training and 20% test sets, stratified by US to preserve the proportion of US and non-US stores in both partitions.

set.seed(42)
carseats_split <- initial_split(Carseats, prop = 0.80, strata = US)
carseats_train <- training(carseats_split)
carseats_test  <- testing(carseats_split)

cat("Training observations:", nrow(carseats_train), "\n")
## Training observations: 319
cat("Test observations:    ", nrow(carseats_test),  "\n")
## Test observations:     81

7 Poisson Model Fitting

We begin with a standard Poisson regression as the baseline count model.

pois_spec <- poisson_reg() %>%
  set_engine("glm") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

poisson_model <- pois_spec %>%
  fit(Advertising ~ Sales + Income + Price + US,
      data = carseats_train)

7.1 Coefficient Table

tidy(poisson_model) %>%
  mutate_if(is.numeric, ~round(., 4)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Poisson Regression Coefficients",
    col.names = c("Term", "Estimate", "Std. Error", "z Statistic", "p-value")
  )
Poisson Regression Coefficients
Term Estimate Std. Error z Statistic p-value
(Intercept) -1.6781 0.2154 -7.7918 0.0000
Sales 0.0640 0.0081 7.8780 0.0000
Income -0.0002 0.0008 -0.2924 0.7700
Price 0.0041 0.0011 3.9070 0.0001
USYes 3.0264 0.1408 21.4924 0.0000

7.2 Model-Level Statistics

glance(poisson_model) %>%
  dplyr::select(null.deviance, deviance, df.null, df.residual, AIC, BIC) %>%
  mutate_if(is.numeric, ~round(., 3)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Poisson Model: Fit Statistics",
    col.names = c("Null Deviance", "Residual Deviance", "Null df", "Residual df", "AIC", "BIC")
  )
Poisson Model: Fit Statistics
Null Deviance Residual Deviance Null df Residual df AIC BIC
2635.243 1099.468 318 314 1922.498 1941.324

Deviance explained: 58.3%, analogous to R² in linear regression.

8 Poisson Coefficient Interpretation

In Poisson regression, coefficients are interpreted as multiplicative effects on the expected count via exponentiation (Incidence Rate Ratios, IRR):

tidy(poisson_model) %>%
  dplyr::filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  mutate(
    IRR      = round(exp(estimate), 4),
    estimate = round(estimate, 4)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(term, estimate, IRR) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Poisson Model: Incidence Rate Ratios",
    col.names = c("Predictor", "Log-Scale Estimate", "IRR (exp(coef))")
  )
Poisson Model: Incidence Rate Ratios
Predictor Log-Scale Estimate IRR (exp(coef))
Sales 0.0640 1.0661
Income -0.0002 0.9998
Price 0.0041 1.0041
USYes 3.0264 20.6227
  • Sales: Each one-unit increase in Sales multiplies expected Advertising by approximately 1.13. Stores with higher sales invest proportionally more in advertising.
  • Income: IRR near 1.0 indicates virtually no independent effect once other predictors are controlled for.
  • Price: Minimal effect on advertising spend once Sales and location are controlled for.
  • USYes: US-based stores are expected to advertise substantially more than non-US stores. This is the dominant predictor in the model.

9 Diagnosing Overdispersion

A core assumption of Poisson regression is that mean = variance (\(\lambda = \text{Var}(Y)\)). We already observed that the raw data has mean = 6.64 but variance = 44.23. Here we formally test this using the dispersion ratio from the fitted model.

poisson_glm <- glm(Advertising ~ Sales + Income + Price + US,
                   family = poisson(link = "log"),
                   data = carseats_train)

dispersion_ratio <- poisson_glm$deviance / poisson_glm$df.residual
cat("Dispersion ratio (Residual Deviance / df):", round(dispersion_ratio, 3), "\n")
## Dispersion ratio (Residual Deviance / df): 3.501
cat("Values near 1.0 = good fit | Values >> 1 = overdispersion\n")
## Values near 1.0 = good fit | Values >> 1 = overdispersion
test_resid_pois <- carseats_test %>%
  mutate(
    .fitted  = predict(poisson_glm, newdata = carseats_test, type = "response"),
    .pearson = residuals(
      glm(Advertising ~ Sales + Income + Price + US,
          family = poisson, data = carseats_test), type = "pearson")
  )

p1 <- ggplot(test_resid_pois, aes(x = .fitted, y = .pearson)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "orange", linewidth = 0.8, method = "loess") +
  labs(title = "Poisson: Pearson Residuals vs Fitted",
       subtitle = "Fan shape indicates overdispersion",
       x = "Fitted Values", y = "Pearson Residuals") +
  theme_bw()

p2 <- ggplot(test_resid_pois, aes(x = .pearson)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "steelblue", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Poisson: Distribution of Pearson Residuals",
       subtitle = "Heavy tails suggest variance exceeds Poisson assumption",
       x = "Pearson Residuals", y = "Count") +
  theme_bw()

grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2)

What this means: A dispersion ratio of substantially above 1.0 confirms overdispersion. The Poisson model is underestimating uncertainty, which means:

  • Standard errors are too small
  • z-statistics are inflated
  • p-values are artificially low, making predictors appear more significant than they truly are
  • The coefficients themselves are still valid, but inference around them is unreliable

Solution: We refit using a Negative Binomial model, which adds a dispersion parameter \(\theta\) to allow variance to exceed the mean:

\[\text{Var}(Y) = \mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\]

As \(\theta \to \infty\), the Negative Binomial converges back to Poisson. For finite \(\theta\), it accommodates overdispersion naturally.

10 Negative Binomial Model

10.1 Fitting the Model

nb_model <- MASS::glm.nb(
  Advertising ~ Sales + Income + Price + US,
  data = carseats_train
)

summary(nb_model)
## 
## Call:
## MASS::glm.nb(formula = Advertising ~ Sales + Income + Price + 
##     US, data = carseats_train, init.theta = 1.880271893, link = log)
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept) -1.6954887  0.4253573  -3.986 6.72e-05 ***
## Sales        0.0665984  0.0200011   3.330 0.000869 ***
## Income      -0.0005432  0.0019340  -0.281 0.778800    
## Price        0.0042826  0.0025431   1.684 0.092178 .  
## USYes        3.0265382  0.1661688  18.214  < 2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for Negative Binomial(1.8803) family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 870.89  on 318  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 384.61  on 314  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 1583.9
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 1
## 
## 
##               Theta:  1.880 
##           Std. Err.:  0.274 
## 
##  2 x log-likelihood:  -1571.930

10.2 Coefficient Table

tidy(nb_model) %>%
  mutate_if(is.numeric, ~round(., 4)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Negative Binomial Regression Coefficients",
    col.names = c("Term", "Estimate", "Std. Error", "z Statistic", "p-value")
  )
Negative Binomial Regression Coefficients
Term Estimate Std. Error z Statistic p-value
(Intercept) -1.6955 0.4254 -3.9860 0.0001
Sales 0.0666 0.0200 3.3297 0.0009
Income -0.0005 0.0019 -0.2809 0.7788
Price 0.0043 0.0025 1.6840 0.0922
USYes 3.0265 0.1662 18.2136 0.0000

10.3 Dispersion Parameter

cat("Estimated theta (dispersion parameter):", round(nb_model$theta, 4), "\n")
## Estimated theta (dispersion parameter): 1.8803
cat("A smaller theta indicates more overdispersion relative to Poisson.\n")
## A smaller theta indicates more overdispersion relative to Poisson.

10.4 Comparing Poisson vs Negative Binomial

tibble(
  Model    = c("Poisson", "Negative Binomial"),
  AIC      = c(AIC(poisson_glm), AIC(nb_model)),
  Deviance = c(poisson_glm$deviance, nb_model$deviance),
  df       = c(poisson_glm$df.residual, nb_model$df.residual)
) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 3))) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Poisson vs Negative Binomial: Model Comparison")
Poisson vs Negative Binomial: Model Comparison
Model AIC Deviance df
Poisson 1922.498 1099.468 314
Negative Binomial 1583.930 384.610 314

Lower AIC indicates a better model. The Negative Binomial model is preferred when its AIC is meaningfully lower than the Poisson model.

10.5 Negative Binomial IRR Table

tidy(nb_model) %>%
  dplyr::filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  mutate(
    IRR      = round(exp(estimate), 4),
    estimate = round(estimate, 4),
    p.value  = round(p.value, 4)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(term, estimate, IRR, p.value) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Negative Binomial: Incidence Rate Ratios",
    col.names = c("Predictor", "Log-Scale Estimate", "IRR (exp(coef))", "p-value")
  )
Negative Binomial: Incidence Rate Ratios
Predictor Log-Scale Estimate IRR (exp(coef)) p-value
Sales 0.0666 1.0689 0.0009
Income -0.0005 0.9995 0.7788
Price 0.0043 1.0043 0.0922
USYes 3.0265 20.6257 0.0000

11 Model Evaluation

11.1 Test Set Performance

# Poisson predictions
pois_preds <- predict(poisson_glm, newdata = carseats_test, type = "response")

# NB predictions
nb_preds <- predict(nb_model, newdata = carseats_test, type = "response")

tibble(
  Model  = c("Poisson", "Negative Binomial"),
  RMSE   = c(
    round(sqrt(mean((carseats_test$Advertising - pois_preds)^2)), 4),
    round(sqrt(mean((carseats_test$Advertising - nb_preds)^2)),   4)
  ),
  MAE    = c(
    round(mean(abs(carseats_test$Advertising - pois_preds)), 4),
    round(mean(abs(carseats_test$Advertising - nb_preds)),   4)
  ),
  Correlation = c(
    round(cor(carseats_test$Advertising, pois_preds), 4),
    round(cor(carseats_test$Advertising, nb_preds),   4)
  )
) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Test Set Performance: Poisson vs Negative Binomial")
Test Set Performance: Poisson vs Negative Binomial
Model RMSE MAE Correlation
Poisson 4.2961 3.1227 0.6975
Negative Binomial 4.2973 3.1257 0.6975

11.2 Predicted vs Actual (Negative Binomial)

ggplot(
  tibble(actual = carseats_test$Advertising, predicted = nb_preds),
  aes(x = actual, y = predicted)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,
              color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted vs Actual: Negative Binomial (Test Set)",
    subtitle = "Points close to the diagonal indicate accurate predictions",
    x = "Actual Advertising (thousands)",
    y = "Predicted Advertising (thousands)"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

11.3 Residual Diagnostics (Negative Binomial)

test_resid_nb <- carseats_test %>%
  mutate(
    .fitted  = nb_preds,
    .pearson = (Advertising - nb_preds) / sqrt(nb_preds + nb_preds^2 / nb_model$theta),
    .devres  = residuals(
      MASS::glm.nb(Advertising ~ Sales + Income + Price + US,
                   data = carseats_test), type = "deviance")
  )

p1 <- ggplot(test_resid_nb, aes(x = .fitted, y = .pearson)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#4CAF50") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "orange", linewidth = 0.8, method = "loess") +
  labs(title = "NB: Pearson Residuals vs Fitted",
       subtitle = "Fan pattern should be reduced vs Poisson",
       x = "Fitted Values", y = "Pearson Residuals") +
  theme_bw()

p2 <- ggplot(test_resid_nb, aes(x = .devres)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "#4CAF50", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "NB: Distribution of Deviance Residuals",
       subtitle = "Should be approximately symmetric around zero",
       x = "Deviance Residuals", y = "Count") +
  theme_bw()

p3 <- ggplot(test_resid_nb, aes(sample = .devres)) +
  stat_qq(color = "#4CAF50", alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "NB: Normal Q-Q Plot",
       subtitle = "Points should follow the diagonal",
       x = "Theoretical Quantiles", y = "Sample Quantiles") +
  theme_bw()

p4 <- ggplot(test_resid_nb, aes(x = .fitted, y = sqrt(abs(.pearson)))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#4CAF50") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "orange", linewidth = 0.8, method = "loess") +
  labs(title = "NB: Scale-Location Plot",
       subtitle = "Flatter line indicates better variance control",
       x = "Fitted Values", y = "sqrt(|Pearson Residuals|)") +
  theme_bw()

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2)

Diagnostic interpretation:

  • Pearson Residuals vs Fitted: Compared to Poisson, the fan pattern should be reduced, reflecting the Negative Binomial’s better handling of excess variance.
  • Deviance Residual Histogram: A roughly symmetric distribution centred near zero indicates adequate fit.
  • Q-Q Plot: Some tail deviation is expected with count data but should be less pronounced than the Poisson model.
  • Scale-Location Plot: A flatter loess line compared to the Poisson plot indicates that the Negative Binomial model has better variance stability across the fitted range.

12 Summary

12.1 Final Model Equation (Negative Binomial)

\[\log(\hat{\mu}) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot \text{Sales} + \hat{\beta}_2 \cdot \text{Income} + \hat{\beta}_3 \cdot \text{Price} + \hat{\beta}_4 \cdot \text{USYes}\]

where \(\hat{\mu}\) is the expected advertising spend and the variance is modelled as \(\hat{\mu} + \hat{\mu}^2/\hat{\theta}\), with \(\hat{\theta}\) estimated from the data.

12.2 Performance Summary

Final Model: Summary Statistics
Metric Value
Response Variable Advertising (count, 0-29)
Initial Model Poisson regression
Overdispersion Ratio (Poisson) 3.501 (well above 1.0, confirming overdispersion)
Final Model Negative Binomial regression
Link Function log
Predictors Sales, Income, Price, US
Training Observations 319
Test Observations 81
Strongest Predictor US store status (largest IRR)

12.3 Key Conclusions

  • Overdispersion ruled out Poisson as the final model. The raw data showed variance (44.23) far exceeding the mean (6.64), and the fitted Poisson model confirmed a dispersion ratio of well above 1.0. Ignoring this would produce artificially small standard errors and misleading inference.

  • Negative Binomial is the appropriate final model. By estimating a dispersion parameter \(\theta\), it accommodates the excess variance naturally and produces more reliable standard errors and p-values.

  • US store status has the largest effect. US-based stores are expected to advertise at a substantially higher rate than non-US stores, reflecting differences in market size and competitive dynamics.

  • Sales and advertising are mutually reinforcing. Stores with higher unit sales invest proportionally more in advertising, consistent with a positive feedback loop between revenue and marketing spend.

  • Income and Price contribute minimally. Once US status and Sales are in the model, both Income and Price show near-1.0 IRRs, indicating little independent explanatory power for advertising spend.
